segments.text: The broader Swire group. I don't know, Jan, if we had a chance
to provide a slide on the screen, but in shortly-
I'll just share them with the
clerk.
Great. Okay, thank you. In short, the Swire group is a
conglomerate with activities in many areas across property,
aviation,
beverages, healthcare, trading, and shipping.
And in fact, shipping is the oldest part of the group.
150 years ago,
we started many of our shipping activities.
And that includes a lot of logistics and shipping activities here in New
Zealand, both domestically and also internationally.
I've been based in Singapore now. I'm a Kiwi.
In fact, I grew up on Tinakori Road, not far away.
Very good.
Welcome home.
Thank you very much, and enjoyed living next to Premier House for many years when
it was vacant back in the 1990s, and I understand it's different these
days. But
traveled down from Singapore last night along with Ben, who
lives in Singapore as well. I've been based there for 18 years now
and was very interested to attend today's select
committee,
both as the CEO for Swire Shipping,
but also as a Kiwi, as a CEO
based in Singapore with particular concerns
for a couple of reasons. Firstly, I think as running a global
shipping business
in Singapore, it allows us to sit
on a number of industry maritime boards, industry
groups.
And again, Singapore now a major global hub.
Bringing those things together, I think gives us direct exposure to global policy
settings in the space and industry trends.
And the trends we see, which I'll share a little bit on shortly,
I think are in direct contrast to many of the trends that we're seeing here in New
Zealand.
If I may share with the select committee, one of the loudest and consistent
themes that we see at the moment across the global maritime
industry is a deliberate shift around all things to do with
supply chain resilience.
And this is no longer rhetoric. I see in many cases it's becoming quite a
deliberate and a direct response to the pandemic, obviously some years ago,
military conflicts, rising geopolitical tension.
Quite specifically, we see this in two different areas.
Firstly, within our customers, we see the advent of chief supply chain
officer roles,
that became very in vogue over COVID, and many years later,
they are now, I think, a permanent fixture.
We're seeing governments move
to take increasingly strategic and national view of their
supply chains and shipping vulnerability.
If I can give a few examples, in the US,
famously, looking at investing a lot into its own
domestic shipping industry at the moment.
But if we look beyond the US and the politics there, China,
Japan, Korea, Vietnam, all actively
revitalizing at the moment their shipping sector.
India is investing at scale, both-
So just to be clear, this is their domestic internal shipping sector?
Correct. That's correct. And in some cases, that includes shipbuilding, and
some cases it's purely focused just on the operator.
Closer to home, the Australian government, as many of you will be aware, have also
seen the need in investigating investments into what
they'd coin a strategic fleet, which looks to address
risks over natural disaster and broader, I think, resilience concerns that
they may have.
The concern I think we have is against this backdrop, it does appear, at least,
that in New Zealand we are taking a slightly different approach with
heightened vulnerability, particularly evident now with the energy crisis.
It does not appear, at least, that the same recognition and the same importance of
the role that the coastal maritime sector plays on this
overall resilience challenge, and we'd like to share some thoughts on that today.
But my second point, I guess this brings us on to relate specifically to our
interests in Pasifika Shipping here in New Zealand,
and for any other potential domestic shipping operator
now or in the future. We've made two submissions
or many submissions, as you said, Mr.
Chair, and I think I'd like to draw attention today to three
core areas, if I can draw out of those two submissions, and then look forward to
taking questions.
The first point is around Section 198 of the Maritime
Transport Act, which in our view materially favors international
shipping companies. These operators
benefit from scale, and they benefit from
costs of having foreign crews paid substantially less than New Zealand
crews, allowing them to offer lower shipping prices in the short term.
Pasifika Shipping or any other New Zealand flag competitor, frankly cannot
compete over the long term on these terms.
We and many other domestic carriers need to have a level playing field, and that
environment doesn't exist today.
Let me share a few reasons why. Some mainline operators we refer to, or MLOs, as
referred to in our papers, have a significant cost advantage.
We estimate that to be about $11 million NZD
per annum per ship.
This committee, we think, needs to confront this trade-off
directly. MLOs may offer cheaper rates, but
service reliability is notoriously unreliable, and
commercial decisions are often made offshore with little regard, we think, for
domestic issues here in New Zealand.
What we have seen in the past is when economic or operational conditions change,
capacity is often pulled away from New Zealand. This is not theoretical.
It is happening repeatedly.
Secondly, Section 198 perhaps anticipates
that some of these structural differences and therefore aims to
restrict the international carriers to incidental
"volumes" only.However, as committee members
will see from our submission,
this is quite the opposite. A majority, we estimate about
75 or 70 to 75% of New Zealand's coastal shipping volume is now
carried by international shippers exploiting this
incidental exemption.
We submit that when the
definition was first established, it was quite possibly never the
intent of Parliament, of course, for this exemption to dominate New
Zealand's coastal shipping capacity.
We recognize that wholesale legislative review of Section
198 would possibly be a slow and cumbersome process.
We believe, however, that considerable progress can be made through
non-legislative means, and that would go some way in addressing this
unintended consequence.
Useful incremental rebalancing steps that do not require
legislation are set out in our submission.
Drawing attention to a few of those points, we would recommend the clarification of
the meaning of incidental through regulation.
Monitoring of coastal trade volumes, respectively carried by
domestic versus international ships by Maritime New Zealand, which
already has monitoring experience and capability for shipping of all
types.
And then promulgating by regulation a practical threshold for
incidental volumes, above which
consideration should be given to
developing a levy, possibly designed to equalize the
regulatory impost of international and domestic shippers.
So we stress in our submission that these measures do not require
or presuppose any relitigating of the fundamentals of the cabotage
regime in New Zealand.
Secondly, I'd like to draw attention to the Emission Trading Scheme.
The Emission Trading Scheme in a coastal shipping context costs
Pasifika Shipping in the order of $1.7 million per year, while
international competitors, as you're aware, carrying domestic cargo
incur no such equivalent charge. This creates a clear,
ongoing, and entirely avoidable competitive distortion.
We'd certainly urge all parties in the committee to support
moves to establish a level playing field in that respect.
Thirdly, and very importantly, is not related to
shipping as such, but related to the efficient and reliable port
operations, which are a absolutely necessary precondition, we
believe, for viable coastal shipping.
We don't think this is a nice to have. It really is an operational imperative.
If you can imagine a large ship, a large container ship, sailing
to Asia, Singapore, for example, with a transit time of about 18 days,
versus a coastal ship, Pasifika Shipping, sailing between Auckland and Lyttelton
with a transit time of two days.
A 12-hour delay in a port of Auckland, for example, which is not uncommon,
represents over 25% of the voyage to Lyttelton, making it
almost impossible to recover that time without significant cost of
omitting ports, speeding ships up, or doing other things that have a
major impact to customers or the
financial viability of the business.
Whereas it represents, of course, only less than 3% of a ship sailing to
Asia. And from our experience running both in international and
domestic shipping services, that is a very manageable cost
that can be dealt with much more easily.
Delays have a material different cost and schedule
impact. As a result, key ports, particularly in
Auckland, need to be encouraged, we believe, to prioritize coastal shipping
where required.
This is explicitly recognized in some jurisdictions, such as Australia
and the US,
whereas it is not recognized here in New Zealand.
We would recommend that the committee examines this point with the aim of
establishing a framework that encourages and supports
the ports to be able to provide coastal shipping operators with
some form of preferential treatment that are required operationally and
commercially, for the reasons I just mentioned.
We'd also support the expansion of Maritime New Zealand's oversight role
to monitor the efficiency and the appropriateness of port operations
for domestic coastal shipping capacity in the future.
Finally, I would like to acknowledge the positive intent
behind the Coastal Shipping Resilience Funds announced by
successive governments 2022, and more recently, obviously
this year, '25, '26. While structurally different,
I think both have recognized that there are real issues here to be
addressed. Our concern is that to date,
Pasifika Shipping has been largely unable to access the latest support in
2026, despite being one of the largest domestic coastal shipping
operators, for the reasons mentioned above, one
exposed to a lot of the current structural settings.
So in summary, Swire Shipping, as a shareholder for Pasifika, are
absolutely committed to playing a constructive role
in this debate to make coastal shipping viable in New Zealand.
We believe the proposals in our submissions are practical.
We think they're pro-competition. We think they're pro-business.
They're also environmentally sensible.
The committee will be aware that
many reports that have been conducted in the past suggest that coastal shipping is
somewhere between six, seven, or eight times more environmentally friendly
than our road or rail competitors.
However, as shareholders of Pasifika Shipping in
Singapore, we cannot wait indefinitely.
We are facing critical and imminent decisions
around our future participation on the coast of New
Zealand. We therefore urge the committee to consider our recommendations.
And in summary, in particular, that's a regulatory clarification
around incidental under Section 198, together with
monitoring considerations that we have
suggested.Secondly is urgent attention to the role that
the ports play in supporting coastal shipping through appropriate
monitoring and oversight, possibly by Maritime New
Zealand. Thirdly, is a level playing field with
respect to the emission trading scheme, ETS, between domestic and
international shippers.
And then fourthly, in support of
asking for your support in principle for emergency relief to
allow vital domestic coastal shipping capacity to be
preserved in the face of this unleveled playing field and supply chain
concerns.
That is all we wanted to cover today.
We hope that's drawn out the main points from our various submissions.
We thank you sincerely for the time today.
I think our presence here from Singapore hopefully reinforces the
criticality we see of the situation, and look forward to taking any
questions. Thank you.
Thank you very much. I've got a bunch of questions, but I'm going to start
off with Tangi.
Oh, thank you, Mr. Chair.
Thank you for making the effort of traveling back home.
So welcome back.
And thank you for the service
to New Zealand since 1985 in terms of domestic freight that you've
identified in your submission. As you
point out, last year the decision was made
by the government that did clarify the intent under
Section
1981C and purely around that protection of
local commercial interests.
So with that in mind,
my question is about capacity. So if
there is a
regulatory change that would effectively see that just under
48% of cargo being carried by those MLOs,
that still needs to be carried. But what's the sort of focus on
capacity from non-MLOs to be able to continue to
be able to deliver that or take up that uplift in
capacity?
I think to some extent the suggestions that we
are making with respect to the Section 198 itself, but also
the suggestions around the ports go hand in hand.
In order for coastal shipping to provide more capacity,
either through more competitive
entrance to the market or through Pacifica as the incumbent increasing
the size of its ship or the number of ships to increase the frequency of
service, we need
support from the ports. More space in the ports allows bigger ships or more ships
to be calling into the ports. So unless we can
address both the port issue and the definition of incremental hand in
hand, then it's difficult to build the capacity around coastal shipping,
which is one of the key reasons we try to bring these two
issues together. But if I could think
far beyond that to a situation where the ports,
in the case of Pacifica we talk about Auckland and Lyttelton, but I think it really
applies to any of the ports.
As happens in many other countries around the world, if there is a framework that
allows them to prioritize coastal shipping,
then we would quickly see an
increase in frequency of ships. At the moment, coastal
shipping is about once per week. Many rail options
per day, many trucks options per hour.
So the best way to increase that frequency would be to have a ship
two times a week or three times a week, ultimately one ship
a day between Auckland and Lyttelton.
The key constraint to that is access to port space
and access to the changes to the incident
definition.
Can I just have one other question?
And you touched on the issue of service reliability
for the MLOs.
My question is what, if any, implications are there for your
operations when you have a significant amount of
domestic coastal shipping being carried by MLOs that
have scheduling issues around service reliability?
What, if any, of the implications are-- because as part of this inquiry we've
become very attuned to slots and times and all of this sort of stuff.
Mm-hmm.
So if there is this unreliability, what are the
implications for your operations, if any?
Well, if I perhaps can answer that through the
eyes of what many of our customers tell us, who are
using both Pacifica Shipping as often the backbone to their
operation, but then using some of their incremental volumes on the
mainline carriers.
It's exactly that. They need
for their own supply chain resilience to have certainty for whatever
percentage of their cargo it is, and they know that Pacifica Shipping is there
every week.
What they have seen through the use of the mainline operators then is that there
can be periods of a few months or longer where there are
several options per week. The frequency is high, the price may be very
competitive,
but then things change overnight.
And so from a Pacifica Shipping perspective, answering your question, we find that
very difficult to deal with because we cannot scale up and scale down very
quickly to accommodate the flip-flopping of volumes that
occurs sometimes. So it's difficult for Pacifica as well.
If we had more certainty
around... Sorry, let me rephrase that.
If we could also avoid the unreliability,
then Pacifica has more certainty
to the extent that it can upsize its size of ship, its frequency of ship,
to provide more and more frequency, or more and more capacity.
I guess my question is also around
if you've got an MLO that's scheduled into a particular port, and the
reliability of that service around timeliness is not accurate,
does that have implications, for example-
... that you need toYou know, wait before you can come in or you have
to leave early and you're not at, those sorts of things.
Sorry, I missed that point. But absolutely, the answer to that is yes.
It's probably one of our highest frustrations,
is again, not being afforded any preferential treatment.
Oftentimes will mean that ships need to wait at Auckland or Tauranga or Lyttelton
for hours or days, I think, Jan, on many occasions behind
a coastal ship. Which is why we draw attention in this brief summary before to
the importance of an hour lost
for Pacifica Shipping is
a major issue. An hour lost for someone that's got an 18-day voyage into
Asia-
Yeah
... is far less of an issue.
Okay. Thank you.
Okay. Dan's asked to go down the list, so I'll go next with a few
questions. Just in terms of the
model as it works at the moment with an MLO, can you just walk us through this?
Because what we're seeing is,
and the group that we haven't yet heard from is
the major shipping lines. So, and we're trying to get
them in as well because that's an important part of the puzzle.
So we know that we're getting larger ships coming, which
some of our ports can't take. So
I'm really interested in how, because what you're talking about is you've got
competition for these MLOs coming in and essentially hopping their way through the
country. So I'd like you to talk us through how that works, both what they're
dropping off, but also what they're picking up and potentially dropping off
somewhere else within New Zealand.
So, to what degree are they simply bringing product in from
overseas and dropping it at various different ports?
Mm-hmm.
And to what extent are they picking things up from a New Zealand port and then
dropping it off at another New Zealand port-
Mm-hmm
... without, so that they're doing that.
Second thing is, in terms of the hub and spoke model, which you've talked about,
as you get those bigger ships, that's going to drive us towards more hub and spoke,
which I would have thought is an opportunity for Pacifica,
because they can't do it. So can you just walk us
through how that works?
And also you've mentioned in one of your submissions
having one port in the north and one port in the south.
I just wondered how that works because I can see some real challenges in
that. And also challenges, you mentioned resilience, challenges in terms of
resilience. I mean, for example, if there was one port in the South Island that was
Lyttelton-
Mm-hmm
... and we had another earthquake-
Mm-hmm
... we've got a bit of a problem.
Mm-hmm.
So how do we work that
model as well? So there's three or four questions in there.
Sure.
Let me firstly share some thoughts on how we
see the
MLO model working firstly. So I think
it's correct to say that,
typically the MLOs will design their service structure around the majority
of New Zealand's container imports being discharged in Auckland.
And the ships will come in, they'll discharge majority of their containers in
Auckland. They'll then move their way around the New Zealand coast
to different ports, and typically that would include one port in the South Island,
Lyttelton or Port Chalmers. And then oftentimes the ships will come back
up and load the export
cargo in Lyttelton, but also Tauranga.
So if we imagine a typical service structure might be discharge your
imports to New Zealand and Auckland, go to one or two other, maybe
more ports in New Zealand, and then finish in Tauranga,
which is New Zealand's largest export port still, and sail back into
Asia.
Where that interfaces, and in the large part, the mission of
the mainline operators is to do exactly that, carry import,
international import cargo and international export cargo from New
Zealand.
Okay.
Where that overlays with Pacifica, for example, is from Auckland to
Lyttelton, where the main line carriers are going to Auckland to
discharge cargo, they're going to Lyttelton to pick up export cargo, and then on
that one sector in the middle, they can compete with
Pacifica Shipping.
So what you're saying is they're picking up cargo in Auckland to take to
Lyttelton?
Yes. And that cargo may be true domestic cargo in New Zealand.
It may be cargo that's been imported and remanufactured perhaps in Auckland
and then redistributed domestically.
So can you just spell out for us what that percentage
of the domestic cargo actually is?
Because that's
the area of direct competition, isn't it?
Well-
I mean, because if they're bringing something in from, let's say Singapore, and
they're dropping the first set of containers in
Auckland, then they've got another set to drop in,
well, let's say it's Lyttelton, let's say it's Port Chalmers, whatever it might be.
That in a sense is them just coming to New Zealand and dropping things
off.
So this is they're picking things up in Auckland and then dropping them off in-
Yeah
... Lyttelton. That's a bit different.
So if we look at the
overall freight task in two contexts, one is the true domestic cargo,
Auckland to Lyttelton. We then look at it secondly as the
cargo that needs a domestic shipping leg as part of an
international import or export, so call it transshipment
cargo. The share that Pacifica has versus
global carriers of the domestic cargo yard is about
Mm-hmm.
The share that Pacifica has of the international
transshipment cargo, I think is less than 10%. Yeah.
And that brings me on, Mr. Chair, to the second part of my response to your
question, which is the model, what role does
Pacifica Shipping play in the broader international
context? And it is very common. In fact, it's
an emerging trend globally, and we see in New
Zealand at the moment for many shipping companies to move to a hub and spoke
method. And I know it's been talked about in many committee submissions as well,
and we see that in New Zealand as well.
Pacifica Shipping's role is quite different year on
year, but one way or another, Pacifica Shipping's always playing a
roleProviding cargo or moving cargo from ports
that the international shipping companies don't call at, to Tauranga, for example,
or to Auckland, where there may be transhipping.
And that is the second critical role that Pacifica plays.
It allows the MLOs that are both competitors
and partners of Pacifica to be much more flexible
in how they set up their networks in New Zealand.
If I can talk a bit about the resilience question you asked me,
if we use Christchurch, Lyttelton as example,
you are quite right that the service is
heavily dependent on successful operations of
two ports in this case.
However, if we look at the history, the recent history of Lyttelton as well, it is
constantly changing Auckland and/or Lyttelton.
Sorry. And/or Tauranga, North Port as well.
Lyttelton, Yan and/or Timaru. So there are very
few reasons that the shipping services Pacific cannot move between
ports to suit the best regional requirements.
Overall, the design of Auckland to Lyttelton, because that's where the majority of
the volume resolves at the moment.
I think where I was going is that there are
some ports who would try and aspire to essentially cut the other's lunch.
Yes.
And potentially, if you do that, then eventually-
... you lose a certain amount of capacity out of any given port.
Yeah.
Or demand from any given port, they're going to stop operating.
Yeah
Because they can't keep going for indeterminate time if they haven't got enough
trade to do it. Then you don't have that choice as a shipping line of going to a
port which no longer has a container port.
An example, I know Timaru was on your list.
Yes.
If Fonterra decided they wanted to send their product to
Lyttelton or to Port Chalmers,
that container port would
lose 85, 90% of its business.
Mm.
Would stop operating as a container port.
If we look at it from an operating model perspective, this is where the combination
of domestic and international intermodal cargo is
critical for Pacifica's ability to provide the best
of both to the New Zealand economy.
So in order for Pacifica to be a
flexible transshipment operator from any one of the ports we
just mentioned, either because of
natural disaster vulnerability or economic changes that you referred to,
Mr. Chairman, it
also needs a thriving domestic business.
And one follows the other. So if
some of the ideas in the submission
are adhered to, it makes the domestic business stronger, then allows more capacity,
more ships to be added into the coastal scene, which then allows those same
competitors, Pacifica and others, to be more flexible for the international
exports as well.
So just to finish mine off before I go to Simon and then to Dan.
So if we're going down to the more hub-and-spoke model
because there are a fewer number of ports which are able to cope with the large
ships, and you think of those ports, you might say, well, clearly
Auckland, probably North Port, if need be Tauranga.
Wellington might be able to. But there'll be some ports which won't
be able to cope with the bigger ships, and so they will get bypassed.
That
must be an opportunity for Pacifica to pick up
that movement, wherever it might be
from, whether it's from Nelson or
Southport or whatever it might be, to be able to take service to
a final export port.
Yeah, absolutely. And particularly interisland.
Pacific is obviously realistic about intra island competition within the North
Island,
road and rail, but certainly interisland, Nelson, Timaru, or Chalmers,
Lyttelton. I think it's absolutely the--
It is unlikely that all of those ports can accommodate the larger vessels in the
future, so they will need a vibrant coastal shipping option as
well.
So are you trying to
argue essentially that you've got a problem at the moment, but if you look through
that problem at some point in time as ships get larger, as we go
towards a more hub-and-spoke model.
As I said, we've yet to have the major shipping lines in front of us,
the major MLO lines in front of us. That that's a
good place for Pacifica to be.
We think that the domestic component of Pacifica's overall
operating model is key to its ability to work with the
MLOs now and
in the future to be more flexible for whatever their feeder
requirements may be. And so principally, our suggestions here are around trying
to fix the domestic component of it.
Our experience over many years, decades now, has been unless we can make the
domestic component work, it's difficult to be flexible enough
on the international component as well.
So yes, our suggestions here around
the three suggestions we make are fix the domestic side of it long
term. We see the short-term issues we're facing at the moment
are equal part regulation, equal part the economic issues in New
Zealand.
But yes, we do see the hub-and-spoke model is a positive opportunity for
coastal shipping generally in New Zealand.
So your argument effectively is to say
that to the MLOs, you can't pick up freight in a New Zealand port
destined for another New Zealand port.
We think the majority of that needs to move on coastal shipping services, and
incidental volumes of that continue to move on the mainland operators.
Simon.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.
So you've provided some really interesting challenges for policymakers.
Some of the things you're asking for look like protection.
Quite challenging for a government that wants to open markets.
So I'm interested,
what do you think
is the correct policy responseThat avoids
protectionism, but gives you the most
ability to grow your business and deliver more freight
locally.
I think-
Is it the immigration settings? Is it recognizing the qualifications of foreign
workers? Is it port transparency?
Is it a combination of things?
I think of the three things we talk about in
the discussion today,
I think the two that
provide the most impact
on a thriving coastal shipping
long term is addressing and
monitoring incidental, for the reasons that I mentioned before, which refers
to Section 198. So making sure that most domestic cargo moves on
domestic carriers. But secondly, to the ports.
And if the ports are provided with an ability
to prioritize operation, as we mentioned before, coastal shipping,
then I think that in itself will encourage the market
for Pasifika Shipping and for anyone else who competes with Pasifika Shipping in
the future
to operate on a much stronger footing.
Okay.
That sounds quite interventionist.
We've heard from other submitters that some ports'
productivity is much lower
than it should be.
It's been pointed out to the committee that Port of Auckland's productivity is
significantly down on what it was pre-2020.
They've never recovered
from pre-COVID, potentially for a number of reasons.
They failed or attempted automation,
and an increased focus on
workers at the expense of seeking productivity
gains.
It can be debated, but
how much do you think New Zealand's missing a trick when it comes to introducing
technologies like AI and
productivity-enhancing tools?
Well, you're quite right. The productivity of New Zealand ports is low regionally.
We operate international services.
We see that not just in Australia and Asia, but even some of the Pacific Islands.
I think the productivity then in these ports then is
higher than it is in some New Zealand ports.
So we look at two reference points.
We look at that, and we then look at prior to 2020,
as you said, in the case of Auckland, for example, where the productivity was
higher for the reasons you outlined.
Even if we were back to those productivity levels,
the points we make in the paper around having some sort of framework to
go above and beyond that are still critical for coastal shipping.
Okay. Thanks.
Dan.
Thanks Jeremy for your paper and coming before us today, and welcome
home.
Thank you.
Yeah, I guess there's two parts to my questions.
The first is, this is an inquiry to look at improving the
productivity of the sector.
And so I guess one straight off the bat
question is what are your recommendations to us
other than, okay, you've got section 198 and the other
recommendations.
What do you think are the
top three priorities that we should be considering in
our final report to improve the productivity of the sector?
Would be my first question to you.
The three points in our
discussion today are absolutely the three
points that I dearly hope make it through to the recommendation of this
committee.
I hope I made it clear before the problems we're facing at the moment
in terms of the viability of Pasifika Shipping.
We want Pasifika Shipping to be here long term.
We have some very imminent decisions to make this year.
The ship is due to go to a five-year dry docking
which needs to be done in China because we don't have the facilities in New Zealand
in August.
September, we need to take decisions in the next few months, and without
some favorable understanding of these three key policy settings moving
forward,
that's not possible for Pasifika.
Thank you.
So we're very clear about the three things.
There are many other things that I think would be
recommendations of ours as well, but we've tried to be very specific on the three
key ones.
Is there any insight you can give as to whether
Pasifika or Swire Shipping has thought about more
integration along the value chain, so maybe yourselves getting
into the international cargo-
Yes
... so that you are able to exploit the potential cost
advantages that?
So two responses to that. We first and
foremost employ around 60 New Zealand
seafarers on the Pasifika service that have been
a key part of our service now for many, many years.
So it was not our intention to try and exploit the rules around
that. We have looked at ways that given we do operate
international services, not just to Asia and Australia, but also the Pacific
Islands, if there's some way that we can incorporate those services in conjunction
with our employment of the New Zealand seafarers to somehow find a
better solution. But in short, that is very difficult because Pasifika
needs reliability, the customers need the reliability, and as
